stmt: ∀ {Γ : List Tp} {s : String}, (Γ ⇒ # s) → Γ = [# s] ∨ ∃ c : Cand, c ∈ candidates Γ ∧ match c with | Cand.rdiv L B A Δ Λ => (Δ ⇒ A) ∧ (L ++ [B] ++ Λ ⇒ # s) | Cand.ldiv Γ₁ Δ A B R => (Δ ⇒ A) ∧ (Γ₁ ++ [B] ++ R ⇒ # s)
∀ {A : Tp} {s : String}, ([A] ⇒ # s) ↔ A = # s
∀ {s : String} {A : Tp}, ([# s] ⇒ A) ∧ ¬ is_atom A → (∃ B C : Tp, A = (B ⧹ C) ∧ ([B, # s] ⇒ C)) ∨ (∃ B C : Tp, A = (C ⧸ B) ∧ ([# s, B] ⇒ C))

∀ {Γ : List Tp} {A : Tp}, (∀ x ∈ Γ, is_atom x) → ((Γ ⇒ A) ↔ (∃ s : String, A = # s ∧ Γ = [# s]) ∨ (Γ ≠ [] ∧ ∃ B C : Tp, A = (B ⧹ C) ∧ ([B] ++ Γ ⇒ C)) ∨ (Γ ≠ [] ∧ ∃ B C : Tp, A = (C ⧸ B) ∧ (Γ ++ [B] ⇒ C)))

Introduce an inductive predicate `AtomicCandidateTree : List Tp → String → Prop` with a base case for `[# s]` and recursive cases indexed by `c ∈ candidates Γ`, requiring exactly the two recursive premises attached to that candidate, and prove: `AtomicCandidateTree Γ s ↔ (Γ ⇒ # s)`.

Define a recursive support predicate `support_ok : List Tp -> Tp -> Prop` by `support_ok Γ (# s) := ∃ B ∈ Γ, occurs_atom s B`, `support_ok Γ (B ⧹ C) := Γ ≠ [] ∧ support_ok ([B] ++ Γ) C`, and `support_ok Γ (C ⧸ B) := Γ ≠ [] ∧ support_ok (Γ ++ [B]) C`. Prove that every derivable sequent satisfies it: `∀ {Γ : List Tp} {A : Tp}, (Γ ⇒ A) -> support_ok Γ A`.
Define the least context-succedent closure generated by singleton self-support and the three sequent rules, and prove that it coincides with support_ok.

Define an inductive predicate `CandidateTree : List Tp → Tp → Prop` by combining the new atomic-case constructors with one constructor for each right-introduction rule, and prove `CandidateTree Γ A ↔ (Γ ⇒ A)` for all `Γ` and `A`.

Prove full one-sided conservativity: if every formula in `Γ` and `A` lies in the left-only fragment, then `Γ ⇒ A` in the full product-free calculus iff the translated sequent is derivable in the left-only calculus; dually for the right-only fragment.

Define a recursive handedness-sensitive closure for arbitrary succedents by combining the atomic exposure predicate with the two succedent-division clauses, and prove that for every Γ and A this refined closure is equivalent to Γ ⇒ A.

Define the deterministic residual atomic state obtained from a sequent `(Γ, A)` by recursively peeling the outer succedent divisions of `A`, pushing each left- or right-division argument into the context and recording the required nonemptiness side conditions. Prove that `HandedSupportClosure Γ A` holds exactly when this peeling process ends in a residual state `(Δ, #s)` with `AtomicCandidateTree Δ s`, and that `SupportClosure Γ A` holds exactly when the same residual state has `∃ B ∈ Δ, occurs_atom s B`.

Define left-only and right-only analogues of `HandedSupportClosure` inside the one-sided fragment calculi, and prove that for any left-only sequent, respectively right-only sequent, the obvious translation preserves and reflects the handed closure.

Define the canonical reconstruction that re-inserts the uniquely determined outer `⧹` and `⧸` succedent peels of `A` above an atomic residual witness, and prove that for every `Γ` and `A` it gives a bijection between `CandidateTree Γ A` and pairs `(Δ, s)` with `residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s)` and `AtomicCandidateTree Δ s`.

Prove a quantitative residual-search bound: if `residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s)` and `AtomicCandidateTree Δ s`, then every branch of the atomic search has length at most `list_degree Δ`, so after residualization the nondeterministic part of proof search is depth-bounded by the residual context alone.

Define the atomic residual transition graph on states `(Δ, s)` generated by one-step decompositions of `AtomicCandidateTree`, and prove finite-state recognition: `Γ ⇒ A` holds exactly when the initial state returned by `residualAtomicState Γ A` is accepted by that graph.

Prove a residual subformula theorem: if `residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s)`, then every formula in `Δ` is a subformula of some formula in `Γ` or of `A`, and the atom `s` already occurs in `Γ` or `A`.

For any Γ A Δ s, if residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s), then every state reachable in the residual graph from (Δ, s) has the form (Δ', s) with every formula in Δ' a subformula of some formula in Γ ++ [A].

For any finite source list Σ and states p q, if every formula in p.1 is a subformula of some formula in Σ and AtomicResidualGraphStep p q, then q.2 = p.2 and every formula in q.1 is a subformula of some formula in Σ.
∀ (Γ Δ : List Tp) (A : Tp) (s : String), residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s) → Set.Finite { q : AtomicResidualState | Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep (Δ, s) q }

∀ (src : List Tp) (p q : AtomicResidualState), (∀ B ∈ p.1, ∃ C ∈ src, Subformula B C) → AtomicResidualGraphStep p q → q.2 = p.2 ∧ list_degree q.1 < list_degree p.1 ∧ (∀ B ∈ q.1, ∃ C ∈ src, Subformula B C)

There exist Γ, A, Δ, s, and a state p such that residualAtomicState Γ A = some (Δ, s), p.2 = s, list_degree p.1 ≤ list_degree Δ, every formula in p.1 is a subformula of some formula in Γ ++ [A], but p is not reachable from (Δ, s) by residual-graph steps.
∀ (src : List Tp) (s : String) (N : Nat), Set.Finite { q : AtomicResidualState | q.2 = s ∧ list_degree q.1 ≤ N ∧ (∀ B ∈ q.1, ∃ C ∈ src, Subformula B C) }

For every finite `src` and atom `s`, a subset of `{ p : AtomicResidualState | p.2 = s ∧ (∀ B ∈ p.1, ∃ C ∈ src, Subformula B C) }` is finite if and only if the values `list_degree p.1` are uniformly bounded on that subset.
AtomicResidualGraphStep is well-founded on AtomicResidualState.
